title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: LCFS Market Strengthens Ahead of 8th November Hearing
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(14th September â€“ 20th October)
  • A total of 480,289 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 1.27x times rise week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades recorded a rise of 38.30% WoW as 1,453,086 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price increased this week to $68.31/MTâ€¯for Type 1â€¯transfers, recording a 3.13%Â rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 0.29% in prices.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 0.03% WoW; on 22nd Oct, it was $62.83/MT. The benchmark price stood at $63.63/MT on 22nd Oct.
  • D3 RIN prices rose by 1.22% WoW, while D4, D5, and D6 RIN prices each increased by 1.43% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The California LCFS market is exhibiting notable strengthening trends, demonstrated by significant increases in trading volumes and credit prices. The timing of this market strengthening appears strategically aligned with key regulatory changes, particularly the conclusion of CARB's second public comment period on October 16th and the approaching November 8th public hearing. This market behavior reflect expectations of a tighter market following the implementation of CARB's proposed amendments. The regulatory landscape in California is experiencing significant developments, with CARB simultaneously advancing amendments to both the LCFS and Advanced Clean Fleets (ACF) programs. CARB also proposed modifications to the ACF regulation, introducing new flexibility measures for fleet exemptions and revising definitions for utility specialized vehicles. These regulatory updates demonstrate CARB's comprehensive approach to emissions reduction, with stakeholder engagement continuing through a public webinar on November 1st, 2024. For a comprehensive overview of the changes and next steps, please read the full article. Growth Energy, as the leading biofuel trade association in the U.S., is actively advocating for comprehensive and scientifically-based guidance from the IRS regarding the 45Z Clean Fuel Production Credit. The association emphasizes the importance of recognizing various emission reduction strategies, including carbon capture and storage, process improvements, and climate-smart agriculture practices in bioethanol production. This push for comprehensive guidance has gained additional momentum with the introduction of the Expanding Clean Fuel Production Act by Representatives Schneider, Kildee, and Brownley, which proposes a 10-year extension of the Section 45Z credit.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

    [caption id="attachment_588981" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARBThe average Type 1 prices recorded a 3.13% WoW rise from $66.24 to $68.31 this week. Additionally, all non-zero credit prices rose by 0.29% compared to the previous week. Type 1 trades saw 480,289 credits exchanged, recording a 1.27x times rise WoW this week, but the total transaction volume for All Non-Zero Trades had increased by 38.30% WoW this week, to reach 1,453,086 credits.[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_588982" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 0.03% WoW, reaching $68.70/MT. On 22nd Oct, the average spot price was $62.83/MT, which rose from $56.58/MT, the lowest point in September. The benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2024, stood at $63.63/MT as of 22nd Oct, up from $57.67/MT as of 10thÂ Sep.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_588984" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 108 trades were reported in the market, with 14 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was at $100.00 for a transaction of Type 2 at a volume of 22 credits, and the lowest transfer by price last week was at $43.00 per credit of Type 2, reported at a volume of 50,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 150,000 credits in Type 2 were undertaken for a price of $53.77, and the smallest volume transfers of 2 credits, a Type 2 category, were undertaken for a price of $56.50/MT.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_588985" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] In September, trading volumes rebounded to 3.00 MMT following a sharp decline in August. October has shown particularly strong momentum, with volumes reaching 4.99 MMT by October 20th, significantly outperforming the previous year's October volume of 3.85 MMT. Additionally, Type 1 credit prices have maintained their upward trajectory that began in July, achieving an average spot price of $66.02 in October, demonstrating sustained market strength.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_588986" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 14th Â Â â€“ 20th Oct, total VPT recorded a 1.60% rise (WoW), with all non-zero prices rising over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices rose by 1.22% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 1.43% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Growth Energy: 45Z Tax Credit Guidance Must Be Scientific, Flexible, and Timely. Growth Energy, the nationâ€™s leading biofuel trade association, continued to urge the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) to follow the best available science when crafting its long-awaited guidance on the 45Z Clean Fuel Production Credit. Specifically, Growth Energy called on the U.S. Treasury Department to quickly issue guidance, preferably in a rulemaking, that accurately rewards the full spectrum of tools available to reduce bioethanol emissions at the plant and on the farm, including carbon capture and storage, process heat and energy, and climate-smart agriculture (CSA).
  • Gevo Secures Conditional Commitment from U.S. Department of Energy Loan Programs Office for its Net-Zero 1 Sustainable Aviation Fuel Plant in South Dakota. Gevo Inc., a leading developer of net-zero hydrocarbon fuels and chemicals, has received a conditional commitment for a loan guarantee with disbursements totaling $1.46 billion (excluding capitalized interest during construction) from the U.S. Department of Energy (â€œDOEâ€) Loan Programs Office (â€œLPOâ€) for its Net-Zero 1 project (â€œNZ1â€) in South Dakota.
  • Duvall Leads White House Meeting to Urge Prioritization of U.S. Crops for Renewable Fuel Tax Credits. American Farm Bureau President Zippy Duvall urged the administration to prioritize American-grown crops in the production of sustainable fuels during a meeting at the White House organized by the AFBF. Duvall was joined by leaders from the National Corn Growers Association and American Soybean Association to urge Senior Advisor to the President for Clean Energy Innovation John Podesta to take action.
  • LPO Announces Conditional Commitment to Montana Renewables to Significantly Expand US Sustainable Aviation Fuel Production. As part of the Biden-Harris administrationâ€™s Investing in America agenda, the Department of Energyâ€™s (DOE) Loan Programs Office (LPO) announced a conditional commitment for a loan guarantee of up to $1.44 billion to Montana Renewables, LLC (Montana Renewables, or MRL). The loan guarantee, if finalized, will help finance the expansion of a renewable fuels facility in Great Falls, Montana, that will utilize vegetable oils, fats, and greases to produce sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), renewable diesel (RD), and renewable naphtha (RN).
  • Vision RNG Secures $207 Million in Financing from HASI to Construct Two LFG-to-RNG Projects in Ohio. Vision RNG (VRNG), a leader in renewable natural gas (RNG) production, has closed on an aggregate $207 million in project financing with HASI, a leading investor in climate solutions. This landmark funding will be instrumental in accelerating Vision RNGâ€™s growth strategy which will result in advancing sustainable energy solutions and reducing carbon emissions. The financing package includes both a $130 million construction facility and a $77 million investment tax credit (ITC) bridge loan, underscoring HASI's structuring capabilities as a preeminent investor in energy transition assets.
  • Darling Ingredients Inc. Announces Agreement to Bring Sustainable Aviation Fuel Supply to Chicago Midway Airport through Diamond Green Diesel Joint Venture. Darling Ingredients Inc., the worldâ€™s leading company turning food waste into sustainable products and producer of renewable energy, Â announced that its 50/50 joint venture Diamond Green Diesel will provide supply of neat sustainable aviation fuel, to be blended with conventional jet fuel, for delivery to Chicago Midway International Airport (MDW).Southwest will purchase a minimum of 3.6 million gallons of neat SAF (about 12 million gallons blended) for use in its operations as early as fourth quarter 2024, with the option to purchase up to 25 million gallons of neat SAF over the agreementâ€™s term.
